title: The River of Life and Final Words

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — "The River and Tree of Life"

heb.gloss: river of the water of life
heb.paragraph: The river of life flows from 'the throne of God and of the Lamb' (v. 1), uniting the Genesis paradise (Gen 2:10, where a river flowed from Eden) with the Ezekielian temple river (Ezek 47:1–12, where water flowed from the temple threshold). The genitive zōēs (of life) is qualitative—this is water that produces and sustains eternal life. The adjective lampron hōs krystallon (bright as crystal) indicates absolute purity, untainted by any corruption. The river's origin in the throne confirms that life itself flows from God's sovereign presence.

The noun xylon (tree, wood) is the same term used in the LXX of Genesis 2:9 for the tree of life in Eden. Its reappearance here creates one of the most significant inclusios in Scripture: the tree of life that was forfeited through the Fall (Gen 3:22–24) is now restored in the new creation. The detail that it produces 'twelve crops of fruit, yielding its fruit every month' combines the covenantal number (twelve) with perpetual fruitfulness, while 'the leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations' echoes Ezekiel 47:12. The Greek therapeia (healing, restoration) does not imply the presence of disease but the ongoing wholeness and vitality that the tree provides.

refs.note: The Garden of Eden contained both the tree of life and a river that flowed through the garden. Revelation 22:1–2 restores both: the river of life flows from God's throne, and the tree of life stands on both sides of the river. What was forfeited through the Fall is restored in the new creation, creating a comprehensive inclusio spanning the entire biblical canon.

Ezekiel's vision of a river flowing from the temple threshold, deepening as it goes, producing trees whose fruit feeds and whose leaves heal, provides the direct template for Revelation 22:1–2. John adapts the vision: the river flows from God's throne (not a temple, since there is no temple), and the tree of life replaces Ezekiel's many trees, concentrating all healing and fruitfulness in a single, symbolic tree.

The curses pronounced after the Fall—on the serpent, the ground, the woman's pain, the man's toil, and the introduction of death—are comprehensively reversed in Revelation 22:3: 'No longer will there be any curse.' The new creation is defined by the absence of everything the Fall introduced.

God told Moses, 'You cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live.' Revelation 22:4 reverses this restriction: 'They will see his face.' The beatific vision—direct, unmediated sight of God—is the ultimate promise of the new creation and the definitive reversal of the Fall's separation between God and humanity.

notes.note: The river of the water of life flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb—a single throne, emphasizing the unity of the Father and the Son. This river is literal, not metaphorical: it is the physical source of eternal life in the new creation, flowing through the main street of the New Jerusalem. It fulfills Ezekiel's temple river (Ezek 47) and Jesus' promise of 'rivers of living water' (John 7:38).

The tree of life—forfeited in Genesis 3, promised to the overcomer at Ephesus (2:7), and now restored in the eternal state—stands on both sides of the river (perhaps as a row of trees, or a single tree straddling the river). Its twelve monthly harvests ensure perpetual abundance. The healing leaves are not curative (there is no disease) but restorative—they maintain the eternal vitality and wholeness of the redeemed.

The removal of the curse reverses Genesis 3 completely. God's servants will serve Him—not in drudgery (the curse's corruption of work) but in joyful, meaningful, creative service. They will see His face—the beatific vision, the deepest longing of every believing heart, now permanently fulfilled. His name on their foreheads marks permanent, irrevocable ownership and identification.

The saints will reign 'for ever and ever'—not just for the millennium but eternally. The original human mandate to rule creation (Gen 1:26–28) is fulfilled in perpetual, glorious co-regency with God. The curse corrupted work into toil; the new creation transforms service into reign.

The river and tree of life restore what was lost in the garden. Calvin would emphasize the Christological center: the water flows from 'the throne of God and of the Lamb'—Christ is the source of eternal life, and all the blessings of the new creation flow from His mediatorial work. The healing leaves for the nations confirm that redemption is not merely individual but cosmic: the nations themselves are healed, their enmities dissolved, their cultures redeemed.

The removal of the curse is the culmination of redemptive history. Calvin would note that the curse was the consequence of broken covenant (Gen 3); its removal signals the restoration of covenant fidelity. The beatific vision—seeing God's face—is the ultimate blessing, surpassing every other promise of Scripture. Calvin quoted Augustine approvingly: we shall see God face to face, and this vision will be our eternal satisfaction. To reign with God forever is to fulfill the human vocation that sin corrupted: not the exercise of power over others but the joyful, creative governance of a redeemed creation.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase potamon hydatos zōēs lampron hōs krystallon (a river of water of life, bright as crystal) combines three genitives: hydatos (genitive of content—water), zōēs (genitive of quality—life-giving), and the comparative hōs krystallon (like crystal). The river flows ekporeuomenon (proceeding, present participle indicating continuous flow) ek tou thronou tou theou kai tou arniou (from the throne of God and of the Lamb)—the single article tou governing both theou and arniou suggests a single, shared throne.

The phrase pan katathema ouk estai eti (every curse will no longer be) is a comprehensive negation. The noun katathema (curse, accursed thing) is rare, appearing here and possibly in Matthew 26:74 (variant reading). The phrase reverses the katara (curse) of Genesis 3:14–17 (LXX), creating a canonical inclusio from curse to no-curse spanning the entire Bible.

beale.source: G.K. Beale, Revelation, NIGTC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beale identifies this passage as the climactic new-Exodus, new-creation, and new-Eden vision of Revelation. The river of life fulfills the threefold water tradition: Eden's river (Gen 2:10), the wilderness rock (Exod 17:6; 1 Cor 10:4), and Ezekiel's temple stream (Ezek 47:1–12). The tree of life creates the Bible's most comprehensive inclusio: Genesis 2 (creation) → Genesis 3 (forfeiture) → Revelation 2:7 (promise) → Revelation 22:2 (restoration). The reversal of the curse (v. 3) is the structural and theological answer to the Fall narrative: every consequence of Genesis 3 is undone. The beatific vision (v. 4) fulfills what Beale calls the 'presence program' of the entire Bible—God's progressive self-revelation reaches its climax in the unmediated, face-to-face encounter that was the original design of creation.

osborne.source: Grant R. Osborne, Revelation, BECNT (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Osborne reads these verses as the resolution of the entire Bible's narrative arc: creation → fall → redemption → restoration. The river and tree of life restore Eden; the absence of curse reverses Genesis 3; the beatific vision fulfills the deepest human aspiration. The detail that God's servants 'will reign for ever and ever' (v. 5) completes the human vocation trajectory: created to rule (Gen 1:26–28), fallen from that calling (Gen 3), and now restored to eternal, joyful governance of the renewed creation. This is not a mere return to Eden but an advancement beyond it: the original garden was a beginning; the new creation is a consummation. What Adam could have attained through obedience, the redeemed receive through the Lamb's completed work.

hist.context: Revelation 22:1–5 completes the New Jerusalem vision with an Edenic scene that reverses the consequences of Genesis 3. The river of life flowing from God's throne fulfills both the Genesis Eden river (Gen 2:10) and Ezekiel's temple river (Ezek 47:1–12), while the tree of life restores what was lost when Adam and Eve were expelled from the garden (Gen 3:22–24). The tree's twelve-fold fruitfulness and healing leaves extend the Ezekielian vision (Ezek 47:12), where trees along the temple river bore fruit monthly and their leaves were 'for healing.' The declaration 'No longer will there be any curse' (v. 3) directly reverses Genesis 3:14–19: the curse on the ground, the toil, the pain, and the enmity are all abolished. God's servants will serve Him (v. 3), see His face (v. 4)—the beatific vision that Moses was denied (Exod 33:20) is now the permanent experience of the redeemed—and bear His name on their foreheads (v. 4), the seal of permanent, irrevocable belonging. The final statement, 'they will reign for ever and ever' (v. 5), completes the trajectory of human vocation: created to rule (Gen 1:26–28), fallen from that calling, and now restored to eternal co-regency with God. The entire Bible, from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22:5, is the story of humanity's vocation lost and restored.

heb.paragraph: The phrase erchomai tachy (I am coming soon) appears three times in this chapter (vv. 7, 12, 20), creating a threefold emphatic declaration of Christ's imminent return. The adverb tachy (soon, quickly) has generated extensive debate: does it mean 'soon' (temporal imminence) or 'quickly' (manner of arrival—suddenly, without warning)? The word admits both senses in Koine Greek. The theological implication is that the church lives in constant expectation of Christ's return, whether that return is temporally imminent or will arrive suddenly when it comes. The present tense erchomai (I am coming) rather than the future eleusomai (I will come) intensifies the sense of ongoing approach.

refs.note: The opening of Revelation—'The revelation from Jesus Christ... Blessed is the one who reads aloud the words of this prophecy'—is echoed in the epilogue (22:6–7), creating a comprehensive inclusio that frames the entire book. The first and sixth beatitudes mirror each other, confirming that Revelation is meant to be read, heard, and obeyed as prophetic Scripture.

Daniel was told to 'roll up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end.' John is told the opposite: 'Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near' (Rev 22:10). The contrast indicates that what was future in Daniel's day has drawn near in John's—the eschatological clock has advanced, and the prophecy demands immediate, not deferred, attention.

Moses commanded Israel, 'Do not add to what I command you and do not subtract from it.' Revelation 22:18–19 applies this same canonical-integrity formula to the Apocalypse, placing it under the same divine authority as Torah and warning against any alteration of its content.

notes.note: The angel's authentication—'these words are trustworthy and true'—and Christ's declaration 'I am coming soon'—ground the entire book in divine reliability and eschatological urgency. The sixth beatitude echoes the first (1:3), framing the book as a prophecy that demands obedient response, not merely intellectual engagement.

John's second attempt to worship the angel demonstrates the power of visionary experience to overwhelm even an apostle's judgment. The angel's refusal is categorical: 'Worship God!' No created being, however glorious, may receive the worship that belongs to God alone. This is the final, definitive anti-idolatry statement of the Apocalypse.

The instruction not to seal the prophecy contrasts with Daniel 12:4 and indicates that the events described are imminent—they concern the reader's own generation and every subsequent generation, not a distant future. Verse 11's enigmatic statement—'Let the one who does wrong continue to do wrong... let the holy person continue to be holy'—does not endorse sin but declares that the time for moral change is running out. When Christ returns, each person's character will be fixed for eternity.

Christ brings His reward with Him—judgment is not deferred but accompanies His return. The Alpha and Omega title (also used in 1:8 and 21:6) identifies Christ with the God who is the beginning and end of all things. This is one of the clearest statements of Christ's deity in Revelation.

The seventh and final beatitude—'Blessed are those who wash their robes'—is a variant reading preferred in the critical text over 'Blessed are those who do His commandments' (KJV tradition). Both readings affirm that access to the tree of life and entry through the gates require the cleansing of Christ's blood (7:14). Those 'outside' are permanently excluded, not because the gates are closed (21:25) but because their character is incompatible with the city's holiness.

The authentication of the prophecy as 'trustworthy and true' is the final appeal to the reader's faith. Calvin would insist that the authority of Scripture rests not on human verification but on divine attestation—the same Spirit who inspired the words confirms their truth in the hearts of believers. The angel's refusal of worship is Calvin's lifelong theme distilled to its essence: the creature must never receive what belongs to the Creator, no matter how exalted the creature or how overwhelmed the worshipper.

The contrast with Daniel's sealed prophecy indicates that the church lives in the 'already' of eschatological fulfillment: what Daniel's generation awaited, the church possesses. The fixity of moral character (v. 11) is not fatalism but urgency—the time for repentance is always 'now,' because the moment of Christ's return will fix each person's trajectory eternally. Calvin would see here the pastoral heart of apocalyptic literature: not to satisfy curiosity about the future but to provoke faithfulness in the present.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase erchomai tachy (I am coming quickly/soon) has been debated since the earliest interpretive tradition. The adverb tachy can indicate temporal nearness (soon) or manner (quickly, suddenly). The present tense erchomai is futuristic present, used for events so certain they can be expressed as already occurring. The NET note observes that 'quickly' may be preferred, emphasizing suddenness rather than chronological proximity.

The critical text reads hoi plynontes tas stolas autōn (those who wash their robes), while the Byzantine text reads hoi poiountes tas entolas autou (those who do his commandments). The variant likely arose from aural confusion between plynontes/poiountes and stolas/entolas. The Alexandrian reading (wash their robes) has stronger external support and fits the broader context of Revelation's robe-washing imagery (7:14).

beale.source: G.K. Beale, Revelation, NIGTC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beale reads the epilogue as the hermeneutical key to the entire book: the inclusio with 1:1–3 frames Revelation as prophetic Scripture demanding obedient response. The command not to seal the prophecy (v. 10) reverses Daniel 12:4 and declares that the eschatological age has begun—what was future in Daniel's day is present in John's. The threefold 'I am coming soon' (vv. 7, 12, 20) sustains the imminence expectation that has characterized the church since its founding, while the fixity declaration of verse 11 reflects the apocalyptic principle that the final separation between righteous and wicked is approaching and, when it comes, will be permanent. The canonical warning (vv. 18–19) places Revelation under Deuteronomic authority, claiming for the Apocalypse the same canonical status as Torah.

osborne.source: Grant R. Osborne, Revelation, BECNT (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Osborne structures the epilogue as a series of alternating voices: angelic authentication (vv. 6, 8–9), Christ's declarations (vv. 7, 12–13), and narrative commentary (vv. 8, 10–11, 14–15). This polyvocal structure mirrors the prologue (1:1–8) and creates a sense of multiple witnesses attesting to the book's truth. The unsealing command (v. 10) is theologically critical: unlike Daniel's sealed prophecy, Revelation is an open book meant for immediate and ongoing application. The church does not wait for the end to understand Revelation; it reads, hears, and obeys in every generation because 'the time is near.'

hist.context: Revelation 22:6–15 forms the epilogue of the book, weaving together authentication, warning, and invitation. The angel confirms that 'these words are trustworthy and true' (v. 6), echoing the beginning of the book (1:1–3) and creating an inclusio that frames the entire Apocalypse as divinely authenticated revelation. Christ's threefold declaration 'I am coming soon' (vv. 7, 12, 20) punctuates the epilogue with eschatological urgency. The sixth beatitude—'Blessed is the one who keeps the words of the prophecy written in this scroll' (v. 7)—returns to the first beatitude (1:3), creating another inclusio. John's second attempt to worship the angel (v. 8) and the second refusal (v. 9) parallels 19:10, reinforcing the exclusivity of divine worship. The command not to 'seal up the words of the prophecy' (v. 10) contrasts with Daniel's instruction to 'seal up the words' (Dan 12:4, 9)—Daniel's prophecy was for a distant future; John's is for immediate application because 'the time is near.' The warning about adding to or taking from the words of this book (vv. 18–19) echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32, placing Revelation under the same canonical authority as Torah.

heb.word: ἐγὼ Ιησοῦς... τὸ γένος Δαυίδ, ὁ ἀστὴρ ὁ λαμπρὸς ὁ πρωινός
heb.transliteration: Egō Iēsous... to genos Dauid, ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos
heb.gloss: I, Jesus... the Root and Offspring of David, the bright Morning Star
heb.paragraph: Christ's self-identification combines two titles that span Old Testament messianic expectation. To genos Dauid (the root and offspring of David) echoes Isaiah 11:1, 10 and Romans 15:12, affirming Jesus as both the origin (root) and descendant (offspring) of David—He is simultaneously David's Lord and David's son (Matt 22:41–45). Ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos (the bright morning star) alludes to Numbers 24:17 (Balaam's oracle: 'A star will come out of Jacob') and to Christ's own promise to the church at Thyatira (Rev 2:28). The morning star heralds the dawn—Christ's return inaugurates the eternal day that will have no night (21:25; 22:5).

The imperative erchou (come!) addressed to the Lord Jesus is the Greek equivalent of the Aramaic maranatha (our Lord, come!) found in 1 Corinthians 16:22 and the Didache 10:6. This prayer was likely part of the earliest Christian liturgy, expressing the church's fundamental eschatological orientation: the community of faith lives in anticipation of and longing for Christ's return. The vocative Kyrie Iēsou (Lord Jesus) combines the divine title Kyrios with the human name Iēsous, expressing the full Chalcedonian Christology in a single address: the one who is coming is both fully divine and fully human.

refs.note: Isaiah prophesied that 'a shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse,' and that 'the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples.' Christ's self-identification as 'the Root and the Offspring of David' (Rev 22:16) fulfills this Isaianic promise, affirming Jesus as both the source and the product of David's line—simultaneously David's Lord and David's son.

Balaam's oracle prophesied, 'A star will come out of Jacob; a scepter will rise out of Israel.' Christ's self-designation as 'the bright Morning Star' (Rev 22:16) claims this prophecy and identifies His return with the dawning of an endless day. The morning star is the herald of dawn; Christ's return inaugurates eternal light.

Paul's Maranatha ('Our Lord, come!') preserves the Aramaic liturgical prayer of the earliest church. Revelation 22:20 translates this into Greek: 'Amen. Come, Lord Jesus.' The canonical placement of this prayer at the end of the Bible ensures that the church's final word, in every language and every generation, is a prayer for Christ's return.

notes.note: Christ speaks directly for the last time in Scripture: 'I, Jesus, have sent my angel to give you this testimony for the churches.' This authenticates the entire book as Christ's own communication to His church. The dual title 'Root and Offspring of David'—source and descendant, Creator and creature—is the most compressed statement of Christ's dual nature in the Bible. The 'bright Morning Star' identifies Christ with the dawning of eternal day.

The Spirit and the bride say 'Come!'—both the Holy Spirit and the church long for Christ's return. Then the invitation extends outward: 'Let the one who is thirsty come; and let the one who wishes take the free gift of the water of life.' Even at the end of the most judgment-saturated book in the Bible, the door of grace remains open. The invitation is universal, free, and urgent.

The canonical warning against adding to or taking from the prophecy is not a general statement about the entire Bible (though the principle applies) but specifically about the book of Revelation. God will add the plagues described in the book to anyone who adds to it, and remove the share in the tree of life and holy city from anyone who takes from it. The severity of the warning reflects the supreme importance of the prophecy.

Christ's final words in Scripture: 'Yes, I am coming soon.' The church responds: 'Amen. Come, Lord Jesus.' This is the heartbeat of Christianity—the expectation of and longing for Christ's return. The final verse is a grace-benediction, ending the most terrifying and glorious book of the Bible with a reminder that everything—judgment, redemption, new creation—flows from the grace of the Lord Jesus.

Christ's final self-identification as Root and Offspring of David combines His eternal preexistence (root) with His true humanity (offspring) in a single title. Calvin would note that both natures are essential to His mediatorial work: as God, He has the power to save; as man, He can represent humanity before God. The Spirit and the bride's joint cry 'Come!' demonstrates the unity between the internal testimony of the Spirit and the external prayer of the church—both long for the same consummation. The free invitation to 'take the water of life' is the gospel's final offer in the canon, confirming that salvation is by grace alone, received by faith alone.

The prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus' is the essence of Christian eschatology distilled to three words. Calvin would observe that this prayer is both a confession of faith (Christ will come) and a petition of desire (we want Him to come). The grace-benediction ending the Bible confirms that the entire canonical narrative—creation, fall, redemption, judgment, new creation—is finally a story about grace.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The compound title hē rhiza kai to genos Dauid (the root and the offspring of David) is grammatically unusual: rhiza (root) implies source/origin, while genos (offspring/descendant) implies product. Applied to a single person, the combination claims both pre-existence and incarnation. The bright morning star (ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos) uses three articles for emphasis, and the adjective prōinos (of the morning) specifies the planet Venus as it appears before dawn—the harbinger of a new day.

The phrase nai, erchomai tachy (yes, I am coming quickly) is Christ's final self-attestation. The particle nai (yes, indeed) functions as a solemn affirmation, equivalent to amēn. The church's response Amēn, erchou Kyrie Iēsou (Amen, come Lord Jesus) translates the Aramaic maranatha of 1 Corinthians 16:22 and Didache 10:6, preserving the earliest liturgical prayer of Christianity in its canonical conclusion.

beale.source: G.K. Beale, Revelation, NIGTC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beale reads the conclusion as the ultimate authentication and application of the entire Apocalypse. Christ's dual title (Root and Offspring of David, bright Morning Star) combines messianic royalty with cosmic significance: He is both the fulfillment of Israel's dynastic hope and the herald of the new creation's eternal dawn. The Spirit-bride invitation (v. 17) is the gospel's final expression in the canon: even after 22 chapters of judgment, warning, and cosmic upheaval, the door of grace remains open. The canonical warning (vv. 18–19) places Revelation under Deuteronomic authority, but Beale notes that similar warnings appear in ancient Near Eastern treaty documents—Revelation, as the covenant document of the new community, demands the same canonical integrity. The final prayer—'Come, Lord Jesus'—is the church's defining eschatological posture: not anxiety, not passivity, but active, expectant longing for the consummation of God's redemptive purpose.

osborne.source: Grant R. Osborne, Revelation, BECNT (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Osborne identifies these final verses as the liturgical conclusion of the book, designed for public reading in the worship assembly. The alternating voices—Christ (v. 16), Spirit and bride (v. 17a), the prophet (v. 17b), Christ again (vv. 18–20a), the congregation (v. 20b), and the benediction (v. 21)—create a call-and-response pattern that mirrors early Christian worship. The final prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus' is both the most ancient and the most enduring of Christian prayers: it was the first church's cry in Aramaic (Maranatha), and it will be the last church's cry when Christ appears. The grace-benediction (v. 21) is the gentlest possible ending for the most dramatic book in the canon, reminding the reader that behind every judgment, every throne, and every cosmic upheaval stands the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ.

hist.context: The final verses of Revelation—and of the Christian canon—bring together Christ's self-identification, the Spirit and the bride's invitation, a solemn canonical warning, and the church's eschatological prayer. Christ identifies Himself as 'the Root and the Offspring of David, and the bright Morning Star' (v. 16), combining royal messianic lineage with cosmic imagery. The triple invitation 'Come!' (v. 17)—from the Spirit, from the bride, and to the thirsty—opens the door of salvation even at the book's conclusion. The stern warning against adding to or subtracting from 'the words of the prophecy of this scroll' (vv. 18–19) echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32, claiming for Revelation the same canonical authority as Torah. Christ's final testimony—'Yes, I am coming soon' (v. 20)—elicits the church's response: 'Amen. Come, Lord Jesus' (Maranatha). This prayer, rooted in the earliest Aramaic-speaking Christian community, is the final word of the church before the benediction. The book closes with a grace-benediction (v. 21)—'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with God's people'—ending the most cosmic, terrifying, and glorious book of the Bible on a note of pastoral gentleness.

themes.note: Revelation 22 balances prophecy and covenant as the Bible's final chapter. The restoration of Eden fulfills the entire redemptive narrative. The open invitation (v. 17) confirms that grace remains the dominant note even at the end of an apocalypse. The church's closing prayer—'Come, Lord Jesus'—defines the eschatological posture of every generation of believers.

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: River and tree of life: Eden restored and exceeded

rows.label: v.3
rows.text: Curse removed; God's servants serve Him

rows.label: vv.4-5
rows.text: Beatific vision; His name on their foreheads; reign forever

rows.label: vv.8-9
rows.text: John's second worship attempt; angel refuses

rows.label: v.10
rows.text: Do not seal the prophecy (contrast with Daniel)

rows.label: vv.11-13
rows.text: Fixity of character; Christ brings reward; Alpha and Omega

rows.label: vv.14-15
rows.text: Seventh beatitude: wash robes; excluded: the impure

rows.label: v.16
rows.text: Christ's final self-identification: Root of David, Morning Star

rows.label: v.17
rows.text: Triple invitation: Spirit, bride, the thirsty — "Come!"

rows.label: vv.18-19
rows.text: Canonical warning: do not add or subtract

rows.label: vv.20-21
rows.text: "Come, Lord Jesus." Grace-benediction closes the Bible

lit.note: Revelation 22 divides into the final vision (vv. 1–5) and the epilogue (vv. 6–21). The vision completes the Eden-to-New-Creation arc of the entire Bible. The epilogue alternates between authentication, warning, and invitation, concluding with the church's most ancient prayer ('Come, Lord Jesus') and a grace-benediction that ends the Christian canon as it began—with the grace of God.

hebtext.gloss: river of the water of life
hebtext.note: The river of life flows from 'the throne of God and of the Lamb' (v. 1), uniting the Genesis paradise (Gen 2:10, where a river flowed from Eden) with the Ezekielian temple river (Ezek 47:1–12, where water flowed from the temple threshold). The genitive zōēs (of life) is qualitative—this is water that produces and sustains eternal life. The adjective lampron hōs krystallon (bright as crystal) indicates absolute purity, untainted by any corruption. The river's origin in the throne confirms that life itself flows from God's sovereign presence.

The noun xylon (tree, wood) is the same term used in the LXX of Genesis 2:9 for the tree of life in Eden. Its reappearance here creates one of the most significant inclusios in Scripture: the tree of life that was forfeited through the Fall (Gen 3:22–24) is now restored in the new creation. The detail that it produces 'twelve crops of fruit, yielding its fruit every month' combines the covenantal number (twelve) with perpetual fruitfulness, while 'the leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations' echoes Ezekiel 47:12. The Greek therapeia (healing, restoration) does not imply the presence of disease but the ongoing wholeness and vitality that the tree provides.

The phrase erchomai tachy (I am coming soon) appears three times in this chapter (vv. 7, 12, 20), creating a threefold emphatic declaration of Christ's imminent return. The adverb tachy (soon, quickly) has generated extensive debate: does it mean 'soon' (temporal imminence) or 'quickly' (manner of arrival—suddenly, without warning)? The word admits both senses in Koine Greek. The theological implication is that the church lives in constant expectation of Christ's return, whether that return is temporally imminent or will arrive suddenly when it comes. The present tense erchomai (I am coming) rather than the future eleusomai (I will come) intensifies the sense of ongoing approach.

hebtext.word: ἐγὼ Ιησοῦς... τὸ γένος Δαυίδ, ὁ ἀστὴρ ὁ λαμπρὸς ὁ πρωινός
hebtext.tlit: Egō Iēsous... to genos Dauid, ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos
hebtext.gloss: I, Jesus... the Root and Offspring of David, the bright Morning Star
hebtext.note: Christ's self-identification combines two titles that span Old Testament messianic expectation. To genos Dauid (the root and offspring of David) echoes Isaiah 11:1, 10 and Romans 15:12, affirming Jesus as both the origin (root) and descendant (offspring) of David—He is simultaneously David's Lord and David's son (Matt 22:41–45). Ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos (the bright morning star) alludes to Numbers 24:17 (Balaam's oracle: 'A star will come out of Jacob') and to Christ's own promise to the church at Thyatira (Rev 2:28). The morning star heralds the dawn—Christ's return inaugurates the eternal day that will have no night (21:25; 22:5).

The imperative erchou (come!) addressed to the Lord Jesus is the Greek equivalent of the Aramaic maranatha (our Lord, come!) found in 1 Corinthians 16:22 and the Didache 10:6. This prayer was likely part of the earliest Christian liturgy, expressing the church's fundamental eschatological orientation: the community of faith lives in anticipation of and longing for Christ's return. The vocative Kyrie Iēsou (Lord Jesus) combines the divine title Kyrios with the human name Iēsous, expressing the full Chalcedonian Christology in a single address: the one who is coming is both fully divine and fully human.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

thread.anchor: The River of Life and Final Words
thread.text: The Garden of Eden contained both the tree of life and a river that flowed through the garden. Revelation 22:1–2 restores both: the river of life flows from God's throne, and the tree of life stands on both sides of the river. What was forfeited thro…

stories.title: Book of Life or Tree of Life?
stories.summary: The KJV reads 'book of life' while virtually every modern translation reads 'tree of life.' The difference traces to Erasmus's 1516 Greek New Testament, which back-translated from the Latin Vulgate for the final chapters of Revelation because his Greek manuscript lacked those pages.

evidence.manuscript: All known Greek manuscripts of Revelation

evidence.reading: Book of life — back-translated from Vulgate
Book of life — perpetuates Erasmus's error
Book of life — follows Textus Receptus
Tree of life — based on Greek manuscript tradition

stories.consensus: This is not a case of textual ambiguity but of a demonstrable early printing error. Erasmus back-translated from Latin because he lacked a Greek exemplar for these verses. Every Greek manuscript supports 'tree of life.'
stories.significance: The episode is a landmark in the history of textual criticism. It demonstrates that even the received Greek text of the Reformation era contains readings without Greek manuscript support — and that the discipline of textual criticism is not an attack on Scripture but a recovery of it.

coaching.tip: The river of life, the tree of life with healing leaves, no more curse — Eden restored and exceeded. What was lost in Genesis 3 is regained in Revelation 22. But this garden is a city. Paradise has urbanized. Human culture, purified and redeemed, enters eternity.

'Surely I am coming soon. Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!' (v. 20). Revelation ends with dialogue: Christ promises, the church responds. The entire Bible concludes with an invitation and a prayer. Come. The story isn't finished until he does. Every generation lives between 'It is finished' and 'Come, Lord Jesus.'

prayer_prompt: As Jesus says 'I am coming soon' and the Spirit and the bride say 'Come,' consider asking God whether that is a promise you long for — or one that makes you uneasy because you are not ready.
